candidate_problems.text: What is the actual basin of attraction of conditional cooperation in the stated repeated, partially observed game class? The conjecture says a 'broad class' of finite-agent systems structurally tends toward cooperation when three inequalities hold, but it leaves open whether this is generically true under realistic noise, deception, commitment failure, asymmetric power, and model misspecification, or whether cooperation requires much narrower institutional scaffolding than claimed.

How should 'preserved optionality' and 'positive-sum transformations' be formalized so they become predictive rather than metaphorical? The conjecture treats cooperation as valuable because it enlarges future possibility and epistemic gain under finitude, but it is unresolved how to measure optionality in bounded agents, when optionality is instrumentally good versus costly, and whether maximizing it aligns with utility over trajectories or can conflict with short- and medium-term welfare.

Under what conditions does improved mutual modeling push systems away from cooperation rather than toward it? The conjecture assumes that representing others as policy-bearing systems supports conditional cooperation, but the same capability can enable exploitation, manipulation, preemption, collusion against third parties, or lock-in of dominance. The open question is to characterize the threshold where better prediction and communication become tools of coercive control instead of cooperation-preserving institutions.
